_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows David Eck's Introduction to Programming Using Java. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the source guides curriculum order only.
title: Packages & Access Modifiers
summary: Organize classes and control which code may use their details.
refresher: A package groups related classes and gives them a shared name. Put the package statement first in the file. Use `public` for code meant for callers, `private` for class details, and default access for code shared only inside the package.

blocks.body: As a project grows, class names and files need structure. Packages group related code, avoid name collisions, and show which part of the program owns a class.

blocks.caption: The package name matches the folder path. The public class name matches the filename `Student.java`.

blocks.body: Access modifiers decide who can use a member. Public is open to callers. Private is limited to the declaring class. With no modifier, access stays inside the same package.

blocks.caption: A private field prevents outside code from placing the object in an invalid state. A method can check the new value before saving it.

blocks.title: Folders and package names must agree
blocks.body: If a file declares `package school.records`, keep it under the matching `school/records` path for normal builds. A mismatch often leads to class loading or import errors.

blocks.prompt: Which modifier limits a field to its own class?
blocks.why: A private member can be accessed only inside the class that declares it. Callers must use the public operations the class provides.

blocks.body: An import lets one file use a class from another package by its short name. Classes in `java.lang`, such as String, are imported automatically.

blocks.prompt: Which line imports ArrayList from its package?
blocks.why: Java import statements begin with `import` and use the full package and class name. The statement ends with a semicolon.

blocks.title: Package and access checklist
items: Group classes that solve related problems.
Match package names to the directory structure.
Keep fields private unless outside access is truly required.
Expose small public methods with clear jobs.
Import classes instead of repeating long names throughout a file.
